title: Marseille soap
subtitle: 72% olive oil, the traditional Provençal blend
excerpt: The Marseille formula: 72% olive oil with coconut and palm kernel to stiffen the lather. The 72% olive content is the historical and functional threshold, enough conditioning oil to make a mild bar, enough lauric-acid oil to produce a firm white bar with good lather.
sourceNotes: Anne Bramson, Soap: Making It, Enjoying It (1972), Ch. 3, Marseille formula history and the 72% rule. Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), Ch. 5, lauric-acid oil balance in household soap. SoapCalc SAP-value tables (v2024): olive 0.135, coconut 0.190, castor 0.128.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 2 kg cleanly. Beyond that, batch in 1 kg lots.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Active work is one 90-minute session. Cure time is 4-6 weeks. Once cured the bars keep 2 years.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a cool dark cupboard once cured. Discard if orange flecks (DOS) or a sharp paint-like smell develops.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oil left unsaponified. Typically 5-7%. Higher superfat gives a more conditioning bar.
The point where soap batter thickens to the consistency of warm custard and a drizzle from the stick blender leaves a brief trail on the surface.
The 4-6 weeks of resting after unmoulding during which excess water evaporates, saponification completes, and the bar hardens and grows milder.
Orange flecks on a bar surface indicating the superfat oil has gone rancid. Smells sharp and paint-like. Discard affected bars.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Twenty-four hours in. The loaf should be firm to press with a gloved finger. Unmould onto a greaseproof-lined board and cut into 130 g bars with the wire cutter. Stand each bar on a wire rack with air space around all faces.

projectSchedule.title: Turn the bars
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks in. Turn each bar 90 degrees so the face resting on the rack is now upward. Even cure across all six faces.

projectSchedule.title: Cure test
projectSchedule.body: Four weeks in. Slice a thin sliver off one corner bar, wet it, and check the lather and pH. Rich, stable lather and a pH strip reading of 9-10 means the bar is ready. Anything above 11 needs two more weeks.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Six weeks in. The cure is complete. Firm, white, mild bars with a creamy but present lather. Store in a paper-lined box with the lid ajar.

content.text: The Marseille formula is 72% olive oil, 20% coconut oil, and 8% castor oil. The 72% olive content is the historical threshold: enough oleic acid for a conditioning bar, and the coconut and castor provide the lather structure that pure Castile lacks. The result is a firm white bar with a creamy lather, milder than most commercial soaps.
The lye calculation at 5%
superfat

: (720 × 0.135) + (200 × 0.190) + (80 × 0.128) = 97.2 + 38.0 + 10.2 = 145.4 g NaOH at 0% superfat. At 5% superfat: 145.4 × 0.95 = 138.1 g. Working weight: 138 g NaOH. Water at 33% of oil weight: 330 g distilled water.
What you need

items.prepNote: refined olive oil. 72% of the oil weight. The defining Marseille ratio.
76°F-melt-point coconut oil. 20% of the oil weight. Provides lather structure.
cold-pressed castor oil. 8% of the oil weight. Stabilises the lather bubbles.
distilled or deionised. 33% of the oil weight.
99% pure cosmetic-grade NaOH. Calculated at 5% superfat. Weigh to 0.1 g precision.

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Weigh 330 g of distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Weigh 138 g of sodium hydroxide into a small dish. Add the NaOH to the water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never reverse the order. The pitcher heats to 80-90°C within seconds and fumes briefly; do not lean over it. Stir until dissolved and clear, then set aside to cool to 38-42°C.
Melt the oils to 38-42°C

Weigh all three oils into a saucepan. The coconut oil is solid; warm on a low heat until melted and combined. Check the temperature with the probe. Match the oil temperature to the lye temperature in the 38-42°C window before combining.
Blend to light trace and pour

Pour the lye solution into the oils. Lower the stick blender below the surface and pulse on for 10-second bursts, stirring between. The 72% olive oil content means the batter traces slowly, typically 3-6 minutes of blending. Watch for
light trace
where a drizzle from the blender leaves a brief trail. Pour into the silicone mould, tap to settle, smooth the surface, and cover with a tea towel. Leave undisturbed for 24-48 hours.
Cure milestones
Day 1-2: unmould and cut into 130 g bars, stand on a wire rack. Day 14: turn each bar 90 degrees. Day 28:
cure
test one sliver; rich lather and a pH of 9-10 means ready. Day 42: cure complete, bars are firm and mild.
Patch test

Patch-test a sliver on the inside forearm for 24-48 hours before regular use. The coconut oil in this formula can be drying for very dry skin; if that is a concern, reduce the coconut oil to 15% and increase olive oil to 77%, and recalculate the lye accordingly.
Storage and shelf life
Two years in a cool dark cupboard. Store on a wire rack or in a paper-lined box, not in airtight plastic. The failure mode to watch for is
DOS
, orange flecks indicating rancid superfat. Smell-check confirms; a rancid bar smells sharp and paint-like, not clean. Discard affected bars.
